summary: This text is an introduction by John M. Cooper to Plato's Complete Works. While primarily focused on the historical transmission, Thrasyllan categorization, and chronology of Plato's dialogues, it concludes by outlining Plato's philosophical rationale for writing in dialogue form: to compel readers to actively engage in the cooperative search for truth rather than passively receiving dogma from an authority.

principles.statement: True knowledge cannot be exhaustively set down in a book.
principles.rationale: Knowledge requires a constant capacity to express and re-express itself in relation to varying circumstances and new questions. A book is static and can only repeat the same fixed words to whoever picks it up.

principles.statement: The truth must be arrived at by each of us for ourselves, in a cooperative search.
principles.rationale: Philosophy is not about a wise person handing down personal truth for others to passively accept. It requires individuals to do their own intellectual work and critically examine their own opinions.

anti_patterns.description: Writing philosophical treatises that attempt to directly tell the reader the truth.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It relies on the vain idea that simply putting words into someone's head gives them knowledge. True knowledge requires a mind's active capacity to interpret and reinterpret understandings, which a static text cannot do.
